tools.name: read_folder
tools.description: List files and subdirectories in a folder on the local filesystem. Returns metadata including names, paths, types, sizes, and modification times.

path.description: Path to the folder (can be relative to working directory or absolute)

max_depth.description: Maximum recursion depth when recursive is true. Default: 3

recursive.description: If true, list subdirectories recursively. Default: false

include_hidden.description: Include hidden files (starting with .). Default: false

filter_extensions.description: Only include files with these extensions (e.g., [".js", ".ts"])

tools.name: read_file
tools.description: Read the contents of a text file from the local filesystem. Supports encoding options and line ranges for large files.

end_line.description: Read to this line number (1-indexed). Useful for large files.

file_path.description: Path to the file (can be relative to working directory or absolute)

start_line.description: Read from this line number (1-indexed). Useful for large files.

tools.name: write_file
tools.description: Create a new file or update an existing file with the specified content. Use overwrite=true to replace existing files.

path.description: Path to the file (can be relative to working directory or absolute)

content.description: The content to write to the file.

overwrite.description: This flags describes whether an existing file will be overwritten. If the file does not exist, this flag has no effect. If the file exists, this flag must be TRUE to over-write the file. If the file exists and this flag is FALSE, the tool function will not write the contents and will return an error. Default is FALSE.

tools.name: windows_shell
tools.description: Execute Windows command prompt commands. Only specific whitelisted commands are allowed for security. Returns the command output as a string.

command.description: The Windows command to execute. Allowed commands: dir, del, move, rename, type, echo, cd, tree, findstr, where, ver, hostname, ipconfig, ping, tracert, nslookup, systeminfo, tasklist, whoami, git, gh, slack

arguments.description: Arguments to pass to the command (e.g., "/s /b" for dir, "C:" for cd)

tools.name: unix_shell
tools.description: Execute Unix/Linux/macOS shell commands. Only specific whitelisted commands are allowed for security. Returns the command output as a string.

command.description: The Unix shell command to execute. Allowed commands: ls, cat, echo, pwd, cd, tree, grep, find, which, uname, hostname, ifconfig, ip, ping, traceroute, nslookup, dig, ps, whoami, git, gh, slack

arguments.description: Arguments to pass to the command (e.g., "-la" for ls, "/etc/hosts" for cat)

tools.name: read_word
tools.description: Read the contents of a Microsoft Word (.docx) file and extract plain text. Only supports .docx format (not legacy .doc files).

file_path.description: Path to the .docx file (can be relative to working directory or absolute)

tools.name: read_excel
tools.description: Read the contents of a Microsoft Excel (.xlsx) file and extract data as text. Only supports .xlsx format (not legacy .xls files). Returns data from all sheets in CSV format.

file_path.description: Path to the .xlsx file (can be relative to working directory or absolute)

sheet_name.description: Optional: Specific sheet name to read. If not provided, all sheets will be read.

tools.name: read_pdf
tools.description: Read the contents of a PDF file and extract plain text. Works with standard PDF files.

file_path.description: Path to the .pdf file (can be relative to working directory or absolute)

content:
## Code Execution Analysis

Let me trace through this step-by-step:

---

### 📋 Final Output:
```
script start
async1 start
async2
promise1
script end
async1 end
promise2
setTimeout
```

---

### 🔍 Step-by-Step Breakdown:

#### **Synchronous Phase (Call Stack)**
| Step | Code | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| 1 | `script start` | First synchronous `console.log` runs immediately |
| 2 | `async1 start` | `async1()` is called, logs synchronously before hitting `await` |
| 3 | `async2` | `async2()` is called and logs synchronously inside `async1` |
| 4 | `promise1` | `new Promise(...)` executor runs **synchronously**, logs before `.then()` |
| 5 | `script end` | Last synchronous `console.log` at the bottom |

#### **Microtask Queue (Processed after call stack is empty)**
| Step | Code | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| 6 | `async1 end` | `await async2()` resolves → resumes `async1`, logs `async1 end` |
| 7 | `promise2` | `.then()` callback from the Promise runs next |

> ⚠️ `async1 end` runs **before** `promise2` because `await async2()` schedules its microtask **before** the `.then()` of the new Promise.

#### **Macro-task Queue (Processed after microtasks are empty)**
| Step | Code | Why |
|------|------|-----|
| 8 | `setTimeout` | `setTimeout(..., 0)` is a **macro-task**, always runs last |

---

### 🧠 Key Concepts:
- **Synchronous code** always runs first
- **`await`** pauses the async function and schedules the rest as a **microtask**
- **`.then()` callbacks** are also **microtasks**
- **`setTimeout`** is a **macro-task** — always runs after all microtasks are cleared
- Microtasks > Macro-tasks in priority
